ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a chrysanthemum tea entry; this record adds honey and chia seeds as modifiers not captured in the base FoodOn term. 'closest_parent' is used as the base tea is present, but modifiers are not.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers herbal infusions; adapted to represent chrysanthemum tea with honey and chia seeds. No exact compound-beverage code exists in FoodEx2 for this preparation. 'closest_parent' is used as the base herbal infusion is present, but modifiers are not.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g (one cup or glass), providing about 120 kcal, 24 g carbohydrate, and 4 g dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chrysanthemum tea, brewed; Honey; Chia seeds, dried (composite estimate)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for chrysanthemum tea with honey and chia seeds as a combined beverage. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries: brewed chrysanthemum tea (FDC ID 171917 — herbal tea, brewed), honey (FDC ID 169640), and chia seeds dried (FDC ID 170554), blended at approximate proportions consistent with a standard 350 g serving (approximately 300 g brewed chrysanthemum tea base, 25 g honey, 25 g chia seeds). The input record values (energy 34.29 kcal/100g, protein 0.43 g, fat 0.71 g, carbohydrate 6.86 g, sugar 5.14 g, sodium 1.43 mg, dietary fibre 1.14 g, calcium 28.57 mg, iron 0.29 mg) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol confirmed 0 mg (no animal fat in preparation). Saturated fat 0.06 g/100g consistent with chia seed trace saturated fat at this dilution.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Honey and Herbal Beverage Analogous Estimate — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for chrysanthemum tea with honey and chia seeds. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per University of Sydney / Atkinson et al. 2008 convention) based on: (1) honey GI approximately 55–58 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS et al., 'International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008', Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283); (2) plain chrysanthemum tea has negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect GI; (3) chia seeds have a very low GI (~1) and their soluble fibre attenuates glycaemic response, pulling the composite GI toward the lower bound. The dominant GI driver is honey at GI 55, which sits at the low/medium boundary; chia seed fibre moderates the effective GI to 55 or below. Per University of Sydney classification: GI ≤55 = low. gi_category is therefore 'low'. Glycemic load per 350 g serving: available carbohydrate = (6.86 g/100g × 3.5) − (1.14 g fibre/100g × 3.5) = 24.01 − 3.99 = 20.02 g net carbohydrate. GL = (55 × 20.02) / 100 = 11.0 (medium GL, 11–19 range).

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar from honey (5.14 g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage format independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, despite very low sodium (1.43 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or omit honey to reduce added sugar intake; the unsweetened chrysanthemum-chia base is low in sodium and suitable, but the honey-sweetened version qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage which independently elevates cardiovascular and blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey is an added sugar (5.14 g sugar/100g) making this a sugar-sweetened beverage; liquid sugars are rapidly absorbed and produce acute postprandial glucose spikes, which is a concern for glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this beverage in its honey-sweetened form; omitting honey and retaining chia seeds and chrysanthemum produces a low-GI, fibre-containing drink that is appropriate for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.06 g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and low sodium (1.43 mg/100g); chia seeds contribute omega-3 ALA and soluble fibre which are cardioprotective, partially offset by moderate added sugar from honey.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage is low in saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium, making it broadly acceptable for cardiovascular disease management; however, the added sugar from honey should be moderated, and replacing honey with a non-caloric sweetener would make this an actively cardioprotective drink due to chia seed omega-3 ALA and chrysanthemum flavonoids.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chia seeds provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 ALA and chrysanthemum provides flavonoids, but honey (added sugar 5.14 g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, reducing the net anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: For rheumatoid arthritis, omit honey to unlock the anti-inflammatory benefits of chia seeds (omega-3 ALA) and chrysanthemum flavonoids; the unsweetened version would be considered RA-suitable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chrysanthemum Tea with Honey and Chia Seeds (菊花蜂蜜奇亚籽茶) is a popular chilled herbal beverage widely consumed in Singapore and across Chinese-speaking Southeast Asia. It is prepared by steeping dried chrysanthemum flowers (Chrysanthemum morifolium) in hot water to produce a lightly floral, mildly sweet infusion, which is then sweetened with honey and enriched with organic chia seeds before being served chilled. The resulting drink has a delicate floral aroma, a gentle sweetness from the honey, and a pleasant gelatinous texture from the hydrated chia seeds, which swell and form a soft gel coating when soaked in liquid. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 34 kcal, 6.86 g carbohydrate (of which 5.14 g is sugar from honey), 0.71 g fat (predominantly polyunsaturated from chia seeds), 0.43 g protein, 1.14 g dietary fibre, and 28.57 mg calcium. The sodium content is very low at 1.43 mg/100g. Chia seeds contribute omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), soluble dietary fibre, calcium, and iron. Chrysanthemum flowers are a traditional Chinese medicinal herb associated with cooling properties, anti-inflammatory flavonoids, and antioxidant activity. Clinically, the honey component makes this a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with moderate glycaemic concern for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension management; the unsweetened base is low-GI and suitable for these conditions. The beverage is vegetarian, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is commonly consumed as a refreshing afternoon drink, a cooling remedy in hot weather, and a light accompaniment to meals in Singaporean hawker and café settings.

How many calories in chrysanthemum chia seed drink?

What are the health benefits of chrysanthemum tea with chia seeds?
Can I drink chrysanthemum honey chia tea if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of chrysanthemum tea with honey?
How much sugar is in chrysanthemum honey chia seed tea?
Is chrysanthemum tea with chia seeds vegan?
What is 菊花茶 with chia seeds?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for chrysanthemum tea with honey and chia seeds as a combined beverage. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for brewed herbal tea, honey, and chia seeds at proportions consistent with a standard serving. Input record values from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with the USDA composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to honey GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries (brewed chrysanthemum/herbal tea FDC ID 171917, honey FDC ID 169640, chia seeds dried FDC ID 170554) as no single approved-source entry exists for this combined beverage. Input values from GrabFood (ai_generated) are consistent with the USDA composite and are retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite beverage preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a combined entry as of 2026-04-17. 3. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite beverage not available in a single approved-source entry as of 2026-04-17. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite herbal beverage preparation not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-17. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: no hydrogenated fats in this preparation; chrysanthemum tea, honey, and chia seeds contain no trans fats per USDA FoodData Central. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: no animal-derived fat ingredients; honey, chrysanthemum, and chia seeds contain no cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central. 7. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace: chrysanthemum tea brewed and honey contain negligible vitamin C per USDA FoodData Central; chia seeds contain no meaningful vitamin C. 8. Cultural metadata in input record incorrectly listed primary_cuisine as 'Italian' and ethnic_tags as Italian/Pizza/Coffee/Toast — these have been corrected to Singaporean Chinese. 9. GI estimated at 55 (low per University of Sydney classification: GI ≤55 = low) based on honey GI from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283); chia seeds modestly attenuate glycaemic response. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' — GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (≤55) per University of Sydney convention. GL per 350 g serving = 11.0 (medium GL) calculated on net available carbohydrate basis: (6.86 − 1.14) g/100g × 3.5 = 20.02 g net carbs; GL = (55 × 20.02) / 100 = 11.0. 10. Vegan flag set to false because honey is an animal-derived product.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected primary_cuisine from 'Italian' to 'Singaporean Chinese'. Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names. Replaced forbidden GrabFood source with USDA FoodData Central composite estimate. Added GI estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database. Restructured health_context with all three required conditions. Documented all -1 and confirmed-zero sentinel values. Populated comprehensive aliases with consistency verification. Wrote LLM-ready natural_language_description. Set vegan flag to false. Added ontology entries with honest match types.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (≤55) per University of Sydney GI Database classification convention (Atkinson et al., 2008). Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving from 13.2 to 11.0 using net available carbohydrate basis ((6.86 − 1.14) × 3.5 = 20.02 g; GL = 55 × 20.02 / 100 = 11.0). Updated gi_source notes to explicitly state the low/medium boundary classification rationale. Updated notes field to document the gi_category correction.
